title: Elote-Style Charred Corn

subs.name: frozen corn kernels pan-charred in a dry hot kadai give the same effect if fresh cobs are unavailable
thick coconut yogurt for a dairy-free version that is equally creamy
a small drizzle of olive oil or even ghee adds a similar richness

ingredients.name: red chilli powder or smoked paprika

steps.text: Husk the corn cobs and rinse well. Mix the thick curd with minced garlic (if using), a pinch of salt and the lime juice in a small bowl — this is the dressing. Set aside.

Place the corn cobs directly on the gas flame on medium heat, rotating with tongs every 2 minutes, until the kernels are charred in patches all around — about 8–10 minutes total. Alternatively, heat a dry tawa to smoking-hot and char the cobs on all sides, turning every 2 minutes.
While the corn is still hot, rub softened butter over it if using — the heat will melt it immediately into the crevices. This adds richness and helps the dressing cling.

Generously spread or brush the curd dressing all over each hot cob, making sure it gets into the char marks. Dust evenly with red chilli powder, scatter coriander leaves on top and give a final squeeze of lime. Serve immediately on the cob or cut the kernels off and toss in a bowl for a salad-style version.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: curd → thick coconut yogurt for a dairy-free version that is equally creamy; corn → frozen corn kernels pan-charred in a dry hot kadai give the same effect if fresh cobs are unavailable; butter → a small drizzle of olive oil or even ghee adds a similar richness
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Charring corn directly on the gas flame is already extremely common in India — this is the exact same 'bhutta' technique. The Mexican twist is just the dressing: swap the butter and sour cream for thick dahi, add kashmiri chilli powder instead of smoked paprika, and lime juice. Chaat masala can replace the chilli powder for an Indo-Mexican fusion version.
